title: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Scheduled run of `UC-22.2.21` with timestamp, field extract snapshot (`risk_object`, `analysis_stale`), and PDF dashboard export retained in `audit_evidence` for essential-entity risk monitoring.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2) is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.21: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Essential Entities.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.21' running on index=risk sourcetype="stash", tenable:vuln, nis2_entity_classification.csv (entity_tier=essential), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Load a test `risk_object=E-TEST-01` in `nis2_entity_classification.csv` with `entity_tier=essential`, inject `grc:risk` rows where `risk_score=90`, and confirm `analysis_stale=ESCALATE` even if vulnerability count is zero.
controlTest.negativeScenario: For the same asset, lower `risk_score` below 50 and ensure active critical findings stay at or below policy so the row drops off when rerunning after the lookback windows refresh with clean data.

dataSources: `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:risk` with `risk_object`, `risk_score`, `review_date`, `grc_status`; `index=vuln` `sourcetype=tenable:vuln` with `host`, `plugin_id`, `severity`, `state` (Tenable TA Splunkbase 4060). Policy cadence from `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` (`policy_number`, `next_review_date`) via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928). In-scope essential assets come from lookup `nis2_entity_classification.csv` fields `asset_id`, `business_service`, `entity_tier`. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) enriches identities when `host` maps to `assets_by_str`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="essential"
| rename asset_id as risk_object
| join risk_object type=left [
    search index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-90d
    | stats latest(risk_score) as residual_risk max(risk_score) as peak_risk by risk_object
]
| join risk_object type=left [
    search index=vuln sourcetype="tenable:vuln" severity IN ("Critical","High") state="Active" earliest=-14d
    | stats dc(plugin_id) as open_critical_high by host
    | rename host as risk_object
]
| fillnull value=0 residual_risk peak_risk open_critical_high
| eval analysis_stale=if(residual_risk > 80 OR open_critical_high > 5, "ESCALATE", "Monitor")
| table risk_object, business_service, residual_risk, peak_risk, open_critical_high, analysis_stale
| sort - residual_risk
description: Correlates GRC risk scores and live high-severity exposure for assets classified as essential, highlighting combinations where analysis is stale or backlog exceeds policy so essential-tier proof stays current.
value: Lets executives defend the essential-entity risk register during supervision by showing machine-generated joins between formal scores, open scanner reality, and the legal tier tag—without relying on quarterly PowerPoint snapshots alone.
implementation: (1) Keep `nis2_entity_classification.csv` authoritative for `entity_tier=essential` and map `asset_id` to scanner host names. (2) Land `grc:risk` nightly with stable `risk_object` keys. (3) Save search `UC-22.2.21` tri-weekly; page risk owners only on `analysis_stale=ESCALATE`. (4) Pair with ServiceNow GRC tasks for remediation links. (5) Archive outputs with `evidenceSigning` when submitting annual packs.
visualization: Heatmap of residual risk by `business_service`, sortable table of essential assets breaching thresholds, single-value average residual risk, 90-day trend line.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Accepted risk entries not coded in GRC** — Distinguish: residual numbers stay high because formal acceptance exists yet Splunk lacks the flag. Suppress: join `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv` on `risk_object` where `exception_state=approved`.
• **Scanner duplicates from dual agents** — Distinguish: `open_critical_high` inflates when two Tenable connectors report the same host. Suppress: dedup inside a macro `m_tenable_host_dedup` before `dc(plugin_id)`.
• **Essential asset hostname drift** — Distinguish: CMDB retired `asset_id` but scanners still use old hostname. Suppress: lookup `nis2_asset_alias.csv` mapping `legacy_host`→`risk_object`.
• **Quarterly GRC import lag** — Distinguish: `grc:risk` timestamps stall while manual workshops finish. Suppress: time-bound exception rows valid **30 days** with owner approval in `nis2_exception_register.csv`.
• **Decommissioned plant still in scanner** — Distinguish: host pings but tag `decomm=true` in CMDB. Suppress: filter `| lookup nis2_decom_hosts.csv host OUTPUT decomm | where decomm!="true"`.
equipmentModels: ServiceNow GRC/IRM module (risk scoring, risk object mapping to CMDB CIs)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, GRC risk register REST input)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, asset model and identity resolution)
Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060, vulnerability detection exports)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC is the essential-entity companion to UC-22.2.22 (important entities). NIS2 distinguishes between essential and important entities (Art.3), with essential entities subject to stricter supervisory requirements (Art.32) including ex-ante supervision, regular audits, and higher administrative fines (up to EUR 10 million or 2% of worldwide annual turnover). This UC must therefore produce evidence with higher assurance — specifically, it cross-references formal GRC risk scores with live vulnerability scanner reality, so regulators can see that the formal risk register is not disconnected from operational truth.
• Lookup `nis2_entity_classification.csv` with columns: `asset_id` (unique identifier matching the GRC tool's `risk_object` field), `business_service` (the essential service the asset supports — e.g., "electricity distribution", "drinking water supply", "hospital patient records"), `entity_tier` (must be `essential` for this UC), `owner_email` (the responsible person for escalations). This lookup is the authoritative source for NIS2 entity classification. It must be maintained by the legal/compliance team based on national transposition of NIS2 Annex I (essential entity sectors: energy, transport, banking, financial market infrastructure, health, drinking water, waste water, digital infrastructure, ICT service management B2B, public administration, space). If an asset is misclassified, the entire UC's output is unreliable.
• Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060) configured to write vulnerability detection exports to `index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln`. The TA pulls from the Tenable.io or Tenable.sc API using access/secret key authentication. In Splunk: Apps > Tenable Add-On for Splunk > Configuration > Account, provide the Tenable.io API URL (`https://cloud.tenable.com`), access key, and secret key. Enable the "Vulnerability Export" input with interval 3600 seconds (hourly). Critical fields: `host` (the scanned asset's hostname or IP), `plugin_id` (the Tenable plugin that detected the vulnerability), `severity` (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Info), `state` (Active, Resurfaced, Fixed), `first_found` (ISO 8601 timestamp of initial detection). The `host` field must match the `asset_id` or `risk_object` in your GRC tool — this is the primary join key and the most common point of failure.
• GRC risk register feed: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk` as described in UC-22.2.6 Step 1. The `risk_object` field must match `asset_id` in the entity classification lookup. If your GRC tool uses CMDB CI sys_ids (e.g., `sys_id=abc123def456`) while Tenable uses hostnames (e.g., `srv-db-prod-01`), you need a mapping layer. Options: (a) maintain a `nis2_asset_id_map.csv` with `sys_id, hostname, asset_id` columns, (b) use ServiceNow's CMDB CI query to resolve sys_ids to hostnames before indexing, or (c) configure the GRC tool to use hostnames as `risk_object`.
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) is recommended for the asset model. ES's `assets_by_str` lookup maps hostnames to business services, criticality, and owners. If ES is deployed, you can enrich the results with `| lookup assets_by_str asset AS risk_object OUTPUT business_service_from_es` and compare against the lookup's `business_service` for consistency.
• RBAC: role `nis2_essential_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = grc, vuln, audit_evidence` and read access to all three lookups. This role should be restricted to the GRC team and senior security analysts — essential entity data is board-level sensitive.
• Index retention: `index=grc` ≥ 730 days, `index=vuln` ≥ 90 days (the SPL uses `earliest=-90d` for GRC and `earliest=-14d` for vulnerabilities). Tenable data at hourly export intervals generates approximately 5 KB per host per day for a typical 10,000-host environment — budget approximately 1.5 GB/month for `index=vuln`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Entity classification lookup:**
Place `nis2_entity_classification.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-compliance/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_entity_classification]
filename = nis2_entity_classification.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Validate the lookup:
```
| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="essential"
| stats count dc(asset_id) as assets dc(business_service) as services
```
Expected: `assets` equals the number of NIS2-scoped essential assets in your organisation. `services` should be a small number (typically 3-15) reflecting the distinct essential services. If `assets=0`, the CSV is empty or the `entity_tier` column contains unexpected values (check for "Essential" with capital E — the SPL uses `WHERE entity_tier="essential"` which is case-sensitive in lookups unless `case_sensitive_match = false` is set).

**Tenable vulnerability feed:**
Verify the Tenable TA is generating events:
```
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(host) as hosts dc(plugin_id) as plugins
| table count hosts plugins
```
Expected: `hosts` should be close to the total number of scanned assets. `plugins` should be in the hundreds or thousands (Tenable has over 200,000 plugins). If `count=0`, check the TA logs: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ModularInputs source="*tenable*" ERROR`.

Key field verification:
```
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln severity IN ("Critical","High") state="Active" earliest=-14d
| head 10
| table host plugin_id severity state first_found
```
Verify that `host` contains resolvable hostnames (not IPs unless your entity classification uses IPs), `severity` uses the expected capitalisation, and `state` is `Active` (not `active` — case matters in `where` clauses).

**GRC risk register:**
Verify as described in UC-22.2.6 Step 1. Additional check for this UC — verify the `risk_object` join key:
```
index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-90d
| stats dc(risk_object) as risk_objects
| appendpipe [| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="essential" | stats dc(asset_id) as csv_assets]
| stats values(*) as *
```
Compare `risk_objects` with `csv_assets`. If `risk_objects` >> `csv_assets`, the GRC tool tracks many more assets than are classified essential (expected). If `csv_assets` >> `risk_objects`, some essential assets don't have risk register entries — this is a gap that should be reported to the GRC team.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.21 NIS2 Essential Entity Risk Analysis`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`inputlookup WHERE entity_tier="essential"` as the driver**: the search starts from the lookup, not from the index. This is a deliberate choice: the lookup defines the universe of essential assets, and the search then enriches each asset with risk scores and vulnerability counts. Starting from the lookup ensures that assets with no GRC data and no vulnerabilities still appear in the results (with zeros via `fillnull`). This is important for auditors — they need to see all essential assets, including those with clean postures.

• **`rename asset_id as risk_object`**: the lookup uses `asset_id` but the GRC data uses `risk_object`. The rename creates a common join key. This is the single most fragile point in the search — if the naming convention differs between the lookup and the GRC tool, the join will produce nulls. Always validate the join key.

• **`join risk_object type=left` (two separate joins)**: the first join pulls GRC risk scores (90-day lookback, `stats latest(risk_score)` and `max(risk_score)`). The second join pulls Tenable vulnerability counts (14-day lookback, `dc(plugin_id)` for unique active critical/high findings). Both are `type=left` because an asset may have a GRC entry but no vulnerabilities (or vice versa). If both joins return null, `fillnull value=0` ensures the asset still appears with `residual_risk=0`, `peak_risk=0`, `open_critical_high=0`.

• **`analysis_stale` escalation logic**: `if(residual_risk > 80 OR open_critical_high > 5, "ESCALATE", "Monitor")`. This is the proportionality threshold for essential entities. A residual risk score above 80 (on a 0-100 scale) indicates the GRC team has assessed the risk as high but it remains untreated. More than 5 active critical/high vulnerabilities indicates the operational reality exceeds what any risk acceptance should tolerate. Both conditions independently trigger escalation. Tune these thresholds after the first month of operation — if every essential asset is ESCALATE, the thresholds are too aggressive; if none escalate, they're too lenient.

• **14-day Tenable window vs 90-day GRC window**: vulnerabilities use a 14-day lookback because scanner data refreshes frequently (daily or weekly scans). GRC scores use 90 days because risk assessments are typically updated quarterly. Using `latest(risk_score)` ensures the most recent assessment is used.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * 1,3,5` (Monday, Wednesday, Friday at 06:00). Time range: use the lookback windows embedded in the SPL (90d for GRC, 14d for vuln). Trigger: `Number of results > 0 where analysis_stale="ESCALATE"`.

Alert actions: (1) For ESCALATE rows: PagerDuty notification to the CISO office with `severity=high`. (2) For all rows: write to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:essential:risk` for longitudinal tracking. (3) Weekly: generate PDF dashboard export and sign with RFC3161 for the compliance evidence vault.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **GRC score parity**: select five assets from the results where `residual_risk > 0`. In the GRC tool's UI, navigate to the risk register and filter by the same `risk_object` identifiers. Verify:
- `residual_risk` matches `latest(risk_score)` for the asset (the most recent score in the last 90 days).
- `peak_risk` matches `max(risk_score)` — this is the highest score the asset has ever had in the 90-day window. If `peak_risk` >> `residual_risk`, the risk was re-scored downward (treated) — verify the treatment evidence in the GRC tool.

(b) **Tenable count parity**: select three assets from the results where `open_critical_high > 0`. In Tenable.io > Findings > Hosts, filter by the same hostname and severity Critical+High, state Active. Count unique plugin IDs. The count should match `open_critical_high` within ±2 (timing differences from scan cycles vs Splunk ingestion).

(c) **Entity classification coverage**: run `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="essential" | stats count as expected`. Compare against `<search results> | stats count as actual`. If `actual < expected`, some essential assets have no GRC data AND no vulnerabilities — they should still appear with zeros. If they don't, check the `fillnull` step.

(d) **ESCALATE threshold test**: inject a synthetic `grc:risk` event with `risk_object=TEST-ESSENTIAL-01`, `risk_score=85`. Add `TEST-ESSENTIAL-01` to the entity classification lookup with `entity_tier=essential`. Run the search. Verify the asset appears with `analysis_stale=ESCALATE`. Remove the synthetic data after testing.

(e) **Negative test**: for an essential asset with `residual_risk=30` and `open_critical_high=2`, verify `analysis_stale=Monitor`. Then reduce `open_critical_high` to 0 and verify it still appears (with Monitor status) — essential assets should always be visible.

(f) **Scanner lag verification**: `index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln | stats max(_time) as last_scan | eval hours_ago=round((now()-last_scan)/3600,1) | table hours_ago`. If `hours_ago > 48`, scanner data is stale and vulnerability counts may be understated. This should trigger a separate operational alert.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Essential Entity Risk Posture`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Essential assets monitored` (total count). Single-value `Assets at ESCALATE` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Mean residual risk` (amber ≥ 60, red ≥ 80). Single-value `Total open critical/high findings` (sum of `open_critical_high`).
• **Row 2 — Heatmap**: `residual_risk` by `business_service` — each cell represents an essential asset, colour-coded by risk score. This visualisation immediately shows which essential services have the worst risk posture.
• **Row 3 — Risk vs vulnerability scatter**: scatter plot with `residual_risk` on the X-axis and `open_critical_high` on the Y-axis. Assets in the upper-right quadrant (high risk score AND many vulnerabilities) are the worst offenders. Assets in the lower-right (high risk score but few vulnerabilities) may have an inflated risk assessment. Assets in the upper-left (low risk score but many vulnerabilities) indicate the risk assessment is disconnected from operational reality — these are the most dangerous from a compliance perspective.
• **Row 4 — 90-day trend**: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk | timechart span=7d avg(risk_score) by risk_object limit=10` filtered to essential assets. Shows whether risk scores are trending up or down.
• **Row 5 — Detail table**: sortable table of all essential assets with drilldown to `index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln host="<risk_object>"` for the full vulnerability list.

Runbook (owner: GRC / CISO Office):
1. Mon/Wed/Fri 07:00: review alert. For each ESCALATE row:
   - Contact the `owner_email` from the entity classification lookup.
   - If `residual_risk > 80`: request a risk treatment plan update within 5 business days. The risk register entry must be updated to either: (a) document the treatment plan and set `risk_status=treating`, or (b) formally accept the risk with board approval and add an entry to `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv`.
   - If `open_critical_high > 5`: request a vulnerability remediation plan within 10 business days. Prioritise by CVSS score (available in Tenable data as `cvss_base_score`). If patches are unavailable, document compensating controls.
2. If both conditions are true simultaneously: this is the highest priority. Schedule a meeting with the CISO within 48 hours — the regulator could cite this combination as evidence of inadequate risk management under Art.21(2)(a).
3. Monthly: review all Monitor rows. If an asset has been at Monitor with `residual_risk > 60` for three consecutive months, escalate to CISO for attention.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Join returns zero rows for GRC data — all `residual_risk=0`.** The `risk_object` field in `index=grc` does not match the `asset_id` in the entity classification lookup. Common causes: (a) GRC uses ServiceNow `sys_id` (e.g., `abc123def456`) while the lookup uses hostnames. Fix: create a mapping lookup or reconfigure the GRC tool to use hostnames. (b) GRC uses FQDN (`srv-db-prod-01.example.com`) while the lookup uses short names (`srv-db-prod-01`). Fix: `| eval risk_object=mvindex(split(risk_object,"."),0)` to truncate FQDNs before the join.

• **Tenable join returns zero rows — all `open_critical_high=0`.** The `host` field in Tenable data does not match `risk_object`. Tenable often reports IPs instead of hostnames. Fix: use DNS resolution in the subsearch: `| lookup dnslookup clientip AS host OUTPUT clienthost | eval host=coalesce(clienthost, host) | rename host as risk_object`. Alternatively, use Tenable's asset UUID and maintain a mapping.

• **`fillnull` masks data quality issues.** Because `fillnull value=0` replaces null with zero, an asset with broken joins will appear as "clean" (zero risk, zero vulnerabilities) when it actually has no data. Fix: add a data quality column: `| eval data_quality=case(isnotnull(residual_risk) AND isnotnull(open_critical_high), "complete", isnotnull(residual_risk), "vuln_missing", isnotnull(open_critical_high), "grc_missing", 1=1, "no_data")`. Filter the dashboard to show `data_quality!="complete"` rows in a separate "Data Gaps" panel.

• **Essential entity count keeps changing.** The `nis2_entity_classification.csv` is being updated without version control. Fix: store in Git, deploy via Deployment Server, and maintain an audit trail. Add `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv | stats count` to a scheduled search that alerts if the count changes by more than 5% in a week.

• **Tenable scan frequency varies by asset.** Some essential assets are scanned weekly, others daily. Assets scanned less frequently will have stale data during the 14-day lookback window. Fix: add a scan freshness check: `index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln | stats max(_time) as last_scan by host | eval scan_age_days=round((now()-last_scan)/86400,1) | where scan_age_days > 7`. Alert on scan staleness separately.

• **GRC tool migration mid-year.** During a migration from Archer to ServiceNow IRM, `risk_id` values change and `risk_object` naming conventions shift. Fix: maintain a transition lookup `nis2_grc_migration.csv` with `old_risk_object, new_risk_object`. Apply in the GRC subsearch: `| lookup nis2_grc_migration.csv old_risk_object AS risk_object OUTPUT new_risk_object | eval risk_object=coalesce(new_risk_object, risk_object)`. Remove the migration lookup after full cutover.

• **Duplicate Tenable events inflating `open_critical_high`.** If two Tenable connectors (e.g., Tenable.io and Tenable.sc) both feed `index=vuln`, the same vulnerability appears twice. Fix: deduplicate in the subsearch: `| dedup host plugin_id | stats dc(plugin_id) as open_critical_high by host`. The `dedup` before `stats` ensures each plugin is counted once per host regardless of how many connectors reported it.
grandmaExplanation: We line up the official "how risky is this asset" score with what the vulnerability scanner still sees for the sites labelled most important, so weaker spots do not hide behind paperwork.
